quality.rationale: 球对称流体力学模型+度规坐标选取与四维加速度的多步几何推导+真实天体坍缩物理场景

origin.question: Problem 16.25. For collapsing spherical stars we can't simultaneously have the three nice properties: (i) radial coordinate is comoving with a fluid shell; (ii) time coordinate is proper time for the fluid; (iii) the metric is diagonal. Prove that we can have all three properties if and only if the pressure vanishes.
origin.answer: The three properties are satisfied if and only if the pressure vanishes.
origin.solution: Solution 16.25. Let $\theta, \phi$ and the radial coordinate $\mathbf{R}$ be comoving with the fluid of the star. We can then write the metric as$$\begin{array}{l} d s^{2} = g_{t t} d t^{2} + g_{t R} d R d t + g_{R R} d R^{2} + g_{t i} d t d x^{i} \\ +\mathbf{g}_{\mathbb{R} \mathrm{i}} \mathrm{d} R \mathrm{dx}^{\mathrm{i}}+\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ij}} \mathrm{dx}^{\mathrm{i}} \mathrm{dx}^{\mathrm{j}} \quad(i, j=\theta, \phi)\end{array}$$where $\mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j}=\theta, \phi$ . If the metric is spherically symmetric, then for $\mathrm{dt} = \mathrm{dR} = 0$ we must have$$d s^{2}=g_{i j} d x^{i} d x^{j}=r^{2}(R, t)\left(d \theta^{2}+\sin^{2} \theta d \phi^{2}\right).$$Furthermore, $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ti}}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{Ri}}$ define vectors in the 2-dimensional $\theta, \phi$ space, so by the isotropy of spherical shells $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ti}}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{Ri}}$ must vanish. Finally, we can choose a new time coordinate $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{t}, \mathbf{r})$ to remove the $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{tr}}$ term, without affecting the comoving status of the radial coordinate, and we have$$d s^{2}=g_{T T} d T^{2}+g_{R R} d R^{2}+r^{2}(R, T) d \Omega^{2}.$$Thus in general we can have both comoving coordinates and a diagonal metric.Now consider the motion of a fluid element. From the fluid 4-velocity$$\mathbf {u} = \left(- \mathbf {g}_{\mathbf{T} T}\right)^{-\frac{1}{2}} \mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{T}}$$we have the fluid acceleration:$$\nabla_{\mathbf{u}} \mathbf{u}=\mathbf{e}_{\mathrm{R}}\left[\frac{\mathrm{du}^{\mathrm{R}}}{\mathrm{d} \tau}+(\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{T}})^{2} \boldsymbol{\Gamma}_{\mathrm{TT}}^{\mathrm{R}}\right]=-\frac{1}{2} \mathbf{e}_{\mathrm{R}}(\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{T}})^{2} \mathbf{g}^{\mathrm{RR}} \mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{TT}, \mathrm{R}}.$$It follows then that the fluid acceleration is zero (and hence the pressure gradient vanishes) if and only if $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{T}\mathbf{T}}$ is a function of $\mathbf{T}$ only. But if that is so, we can define a new time coordinate $\tau (\mathbf{T})$ from the equation$$\mathrm{d} \tau / \mathrm{d} \mathbf{T} = \left(-\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{T} T}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$$representing proper time for the fluid. Thus the three nice properties are all satisfied if and only if the pressure gradient is zero; since the pressure must be zero on the surface of the star, a vanishing pressure gradient is equivalent to zero pressure.
